article_title: Apple Kicks Off Its Biggest Product Week of 2026 With Six Devices Spanning iPhone, Mac, and iPad

content_preview.title: Apple Kicks Off Its Biggest Product Week of 2026 With Six Devices Spanning iPhone, Mac, and iPad
content_preview.summary: Apple begins staggered press-release launches on March 2, culminating in a hands-on media experience on March 4, delivering its most comprehensive hardware refresh in years.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Apple has opened what CEO Tim Cook called "a big week ahead," with the company staging a staggered rollout of at least six hardware products beginning March 2, 2026. Rather than hosting a traditional keynote, Apple is issuing announcements via press release across Monday through Wednesday, before hosting a Special Experience for invited media on March 4 in New York, London, and Shanghai. The week represents the company's most sweeping simultaneous hardware update in recent memory, t...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured 911-word News article covering Apple's March 2026 hardware week across six devices. Clear section headings, good depth on each product, and a useful Analysis section contextualizing Apple's broader strategy. The 'What We Don't Know' section appropriately flags unconfirmed details, which reflects good journalistic practice.
editor_notes.source_verification: Six sources listed; five cited inline. MacRumors, Macworld (x2), MacObserver, and Geeky Gadgets are all verified reputable Apple-specialist publications. The 9to5Mac source (a reader poll) is listed in the sources array but is not cited inline in the article body — it is unused. This is a minor discrepancy but not a blocking concern since no claim is attributed to it. All four Round 1 corrections were independently verified against the original sources.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All four factual/attribution errors identified in Round 1 have been correctly addressed. (1) MacBook Air M5 pricing: now correctly states the M4 Air starts at $999, with $1,099 framed as speculative — confirmed by the Macworld M5 Air article. (2) Front camera Center Stage claim: removed; replaced with the source-accurate 'any orientation' sensor feature from the Macworld iPhone 17e article. (3) C1X modem attribution: correctly hedged as 'pre-announcement reports indicate' rather than Apple's official claims — consistent with Macworld's 'apparently' framing. (4) HomePod/Apple TV attribution: re-attributed from 9to5Mac (which never discussed this) to MacRumors, which explicitly explains the Siri-tied delay.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism, no editorializing. The Analysis section offers interpretation but grounds it in observable product choices rather than opinion.
editor_notes.originality: No existing article covers Apple's March 2026 hardware event. This is a genuinely new topic for the publication.
recommendations: The 9to5Mac source (reader poll) is listed in sources but never cited in the article body. In a future rewrite cycle, consider either removing it from the sources list or adding a citation where relevant — e.g., the overview section noting Tim Cook's 'big week ahead' quote, which the 9to5Mac article also references.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Round 2 submission successfully addresses all four issues raised in the Round 1 review. All corrections are source-accurate and appropriately hedged. The article is ready for publication.
